speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is February 16, 1996. The survivor is Ladislav Jung. The interviewer is Robin Rundle. We are in Laguna Niguel, in the United States. And the language is English. My name is Robin Rundle. Today's date is February 16, 1996. I have the privilege of interviewing Ladislav Jung today, in Laguna Niguel, in the United States, for the survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Can you state your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ladislav Jung-- L-A-D-I-S-L-A-V, J-U-N-G. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I came to United States, they call me Louis. And when I came to Israel, they called me [NON-ENGLISH]. Otherwise, there are not any other nicknames.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birth date was in Presov, August 21, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today I am 68.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city, and spelling, and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-R-E-S-O-V, and C-Z-- C-Z-C-H-O-S-L-O-V-A-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Presov, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Presov, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about your family, when you were a little boy, your home, and your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our family life was great. Mine-- my father was a businessman. And my-- mine mother used to take care of me and mostly had social life. We had-- first my father had a business. Then he joined my grandfather's bakery business. Actually, there was a whole production while we had three shops in town. One was one end, and the other in the middle. And the third was on the other end. And we delivered to military, and to all the organizations, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was a 24-hour operation. We-- there was no stop business. And we had a big crew of bakers who worked three shifts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, the household was very busy. We had two maids, and servant, and we had somebody who take care of me. [LAUGHS] So that was a very good life. And I attended kindergarten. And first years I went to nuns. There was the-- they called Santa Maria. Then the second I went to a Jewish kindergarten. And later on, I attended grade schools what was run by state. There was a state school. And later on, I made a test and went to a gymnasium. There was a-- a realistic gymnasium where it was-- you were prepared for engineering or technical education. And education in Czechoslovakia was very important for young people to prepare for life. So I chose to go to high school to gymnasium. Then the middle of my studies, that third year, Slovakia became a independent state. They-- they-- they separated from Czech Republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your-- to your childhood--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before we continue. And can you tell us some more about your parents and your grandparents, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mine parents had a very good life, and their grandparents too. They travel a lot. And-- and also, we did lots of hiking with my father where my father liked sports game, matter of fact. [LAUGHS] He promoted lots of games. He was like promoter and-- in the city. And mine favorite sport was hockey and figure skating. So I did lots of-- in gymnasium and high school, I played hockey and also did some figure skating. So life was very good. And it was very-- people met each other, and the social life was great. And also, my grandfather spoiled me very much. And of course, like kids, we played soccer and-- and we-- we had a very-- very nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother. He was one year old when he was killed in-- by the Germans, in Poland. And I had lots of uncles who spoiled me very much. There were uncles, from-- brothers from my mother-- and they were living in-- in Czechoslovakia, in the same city close by. There were four of them. And the one uncle, he was-- he was in United States. And also, mine grandfather, he travelled a lot. They would travel to Vienna and to-- to Budapest where they have a very cultural and nice social life. My grandfather also visited, after World War II, United States. Only he returned back to Czechoslovakia. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: After World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: After World War-- after World War I, yeah. I correct. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your grandparents live nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had-- we all live nearby, and we had a big-- big household. And we-- we enjoy life very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of your friends as you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had lots of friends from the neighborhood and also from school who I grow up with. And then the life completely was disrupted during-- during the war and over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: --unrest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish as well as gentile friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had more-- [LAUGHS] I had the most gentile friends where I was brought-- brought up in-- in state schools and education-- I was brought up mostly-- and not with Jewish-- very few Jewish friends. Mostly I had gentile friends. And-- and later on, I was forced to quit schools while there was numerous rules. This mean that the Jewish people couldn't-- couldn't attend anymore state run schools. So I-- I was-- went to training to become a mechanic and also with constructions with iron work. So then also I went to some Jewish school-- only very short time. Later on, when-- when the Slovak Republic was formed, I-- I was deported in 1940-- '42 to-- to Poland with mine parents and grandparents. And we were put in wagons with the-- with the so-called-- there was a guard, Hlinka guard, and then we were deported to Poland. Before we cross the border, we were turned over to SS. And on the way to-- to Deblin, before, we stopped at Auschwitz. We were in cattle carts-- stuffed up, lots of people with no food, and no nothing. And we stayed there on sidelines about six hours, and then came some command. And they took us farther to Deblin. There was a ghetto. And in Deblin, I stayed with mine parents. And later on, I-- they looked for people to work on airport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here for a moment

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue with your war time experience--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back and talk a little bit more about the prewar time. And can you tell us if your family was very religious? Did they attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, they were not religious at all. Mine grandparents visited synagogue three times a year-- two times a year actually-- for the high Jewish holy days. And mine father was even much less religious than my grandfather. And we just-- my grandfather still observed Saturday, only not in-- completely in a religious style. He didn't work. Or-- or-- so only we didn't had too much observance any-- any religious where I completely-- I didn't attend any-- any religious classes or any-- I was maybe ready already like a kid, more agnostic than-- than any Jewish-- Jewish faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family celebrate the Sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe relationships between the Jews and non-Jews of Presov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- till-- till the war, until they separated Slovaks from the Czechs, the-- there was a very good relation. And matter of fact, we-- we had a contact. We were visited many time by the Russian Orthodox bishop and-- and some of the [NON-ENGLISH], that they were family friends where-- my father also was for a time involved with the politics, of the city politics. And he was also one time also in the city council. So there was very good relation between non-Jews and Jews. And there was no-- no problem. Only when the Slovaks tried to separate-- matter of fact, there was a Catholic president who was a-- a Catholic priest. He was elected. And-- and he somehow didn't-- he-- he collaborated with-- with the Germans and Hitler. And after the war, they-- he was executed by-- by hanging. Only a total-- our family, the-- the relation between the Jews and Catholic was-- and there was also a lot of-- a lot of Greek-- not Greek only-- Russian Orthodox Church and members. And we had a exceptional good relation. One of mine uncles, also he-- he was going-- this was before-- I think so-- still in Austria and Hungary. He attended a Russian Orthodox Church around our school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our family was-- was like Jewish and non-Jewish, and we-- we were intermarried with-- with non-Jews. This time, also, for instance, one of mine uncles, he was Catholic. He was not Jewish-- and other family members who were not Jewish religion. So there was no difference or any war between religion, and we took it by the-- how the human being is, not what he believes in. So you never experienced any anti-Semitism as a young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a young kid-- not. Till then they departed, and there was stirred up hate, and propaganda, and so forth. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did know about the war and any danger to your family? What were the first notions you had about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: First notions after the war was when we were occupied also by Germans. And-- and then the-- actually, the Slovaks were cooperating with the Germans. So they pulled back only. We saw the destruction of Ukraine in Russia and also occupation of Poland. So we got some terrible news what's happened, not just with Jewish people only, also with the Polish people and the-- and the Russian people. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --there were horror stories where they put the people in barns and-- and just light them up and burn them to death alive-- so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your family's reaction or preparation for--

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't prepare war. You couldn't prepare for it, I mean. We-- we thought maybe we were so assimilated. This, we thought. They will pass, and blow-- blow away, and will not-- will-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --not happen. [LAUGHS] Only to our disappointment, that was not true. For instance, one of our neighbors was a Russian Orthodox priest who was high up, and he had a beautiful villa. This is a big home. And he wanted to take me in and-- and hide me, or take, you know. Only my mother said, well, if we go, I want my son go with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this were my parents. Then the war-- they were killed. And I, through the hardships of concentration camps, and hunger, and everything else, I stayed alive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As a young boy then in Presov, how did your life change with the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was pretty shocking. While-- [LAUGHS] I couldn't-- I couldn't continue my studies, and we were limited with our movements and so forth. So actually, my life, already like a young man, was destroyed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What other restrictions do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we couldn't move, travel, or we couldn't, by certain hours, we couldn't be on street. We couldn't associate with-- with non-Jewish friends and so forth. So that was pretty sad. Even so, when my friends wanted to associate with me, they'd been looked down. And there was a certain pressure on them to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father business had been taken over. Actually, he-- mine father and grandfather business had to be given up, and we gave it to my grandfather. And he decided to give it to one of his oldest people who work for him. And he runned it. And then we, short time after we been gathered together, they came to our house. And we were just allowed to take what we could carry. And we been taken from house by the Hlinka guard and gathered in the synagogue where they tear all the benches out and just put straw on the-- on the floor. And we had to be there one night. And next day, we were put in the cattle cars and deported to-- to Poland. Of course, for me, it was not-- today, if I look at that-- it was not such a big shock like to my parents and grandparents. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: For-- How did you feel as a young boy having this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: --happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. While we were brought up-- and our roots were actually Czechoslovakia-- [LAUGHS] --I become hysterical now a little bit. I cry. And mine grandfather, he fought in World War I for the country, and he was let down. He was decorated-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --veteran. And he were-- and one of mine father's brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --he he-- I mean, he give the life. Yeah. In-- he was in Hungary in Austrian Army. And he fall in Roveleto. That was on the border of Italy and Austria. And now we-- we were the scum of the Earth, and so forth described, and humiliated, and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --put in the cattle cars worse than the cattle were. We-- there was-- you couldn't-- they pack you so tight, you couldn't even sit down. You had to stand up and-- for hours and days-- spend time with no facilities, nothing-- and transported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about going to the synagogue that night. Did-- did they warn you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: --they-- they-- we-- we got some message. Some people was warned, some were not warned. And some were beaten. With us, at least, they had some [LAUGHS] respect, and so forth. Only some people were mishandled. And-- and they just could take what they see and-- and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took some-- some provision food. I-- we had a-- a-- a-- I had a brother one year old. Even then, he was not one year old. When he was killed he was one year old. Whatever clothing we just could take. You couldn't take any furs or any-- where-- just what you put on you and maybe extra set of shoes or something. And this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember taking anything special, any toy or anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you witness-- what did you witness at the synagogue that night? What did people talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people talk about they were scared. They didn't know where-- where they will take us and where we will go, and so forth. There were some news that the-- that existed like Treblinka where they were exterminating the people and so forth. Only we-- we didn't had any idea of where it will take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me again what-- what time of year, what year this is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what month this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately May. And we arrived to Deblin in a-- in a-- in cattle cars, and they unloaded us. And there was a shock. They were screaming and-- and complete chaos. And they loaded our belongings in wagons-- with horse-drawn wagons. And from the railroad station, where were-- we-- we were going by foot, not by wagon-- only the belonging-- to the ghetto. That was a tremendous experience and shock for us to come to Poland where we had beautiful homes. And Poland was a little bit more back with everything, with the style of living. And-- and the houses and everything-- even the locks-- they were like by us, on barns. They were the door locks where you push. [LAUGHS] And the-- the whole thing was gruesome. So going back, you had to-- we were walking behind the-- behind that horse-drawn wagons. The Polish people who were delivering things, they were throwing their luggage. The people were waiting and throwing. And they were robbing us right there and there of our belongings. And you couldn't do nothing while you had the-- you had the German SS. If you would step out of the line, they shoot you with no reason. So lots of people lost their belongings. And this is what we-- in ghetto there was no work. There was-- we were just living from the provisions what we had in food. And a few months-- about two months later-- they were looking for people to go and work on the airport in-- there was one of the big airports in Poland-- military airport. Then the Germans used the airport to fight the-- the eastern front in Russia, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they need people to work on the airport. And they looked mainly for craftsmen and-- and people who-- who are capable to burden. I was then a young boy, [LAUGHS] and with mine little training what I got home, I knew how to work with iron a little bit, weld a little bit, cut, and measure. I said as I am a-- [LAUGHS] --trained metal worker and-- and mechanic. So they took me for work, and there was a--
